region_of_origin: East Coast of Peninsular Malaysia — Kelantan and Terengganu
food_subgroup: Charcoal-grilled coconut-milk marinated and basted chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Ayam Percik. Closest parent is charcoal-grilled coconut-marinated chicken. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Charcoal-grilled chicken with coconut milk basting (Malay ayam percik style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CHF used as closest defensible parent for grilled chicken preparations. No exact Ayam Percik entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Ayam Percik or equivalent Malaysian grilled chicken preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Ayam Percik is approximately 250g, representing half a chicken (bone-in) grilled over charcoal and basted with coconut milk sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Ayam Percik — IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD) entry for Ayam Percik. Core macros and micronutrients reflect the verified IMR record. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are supplemented from enriched JSON with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Protein-Fat-Rich Foods — Analogous Estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Percik in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with coconut-milk-based grilled chicken preparations. The high fat content from coconut milk (5.2g saturated fat/100g) and high protein (18.5g/100g) substantially slow glucose absorption from the small carbohydrate fraction (4.2g/100g). Palm sugar used in basting has a GI of approximately 35–45. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (45 × 4.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.7g available carbohydrate × GI factor ≈ 1.9 GL units, confirming low glycemic impact. Confidence: 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 390mg/100g; a 250g serving delivers approximately 975mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200g and avoid additional salt or fish sauce in preparation, as a full 250g serving provides approximately 975mg sodium, representing 42% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (4.2g/100g) and low GI (~45) with high protein (18.5g/100g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ayam Percik is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice; the low carbohydrate content (4.2g/100g), low GI (~45), and high protein (18.5g/100g) support satiety and minimise postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.2g/100g from coconut milk; cholesterol 88mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to the saturated fat contribution from coconut milk (5.2g/100g); however, the charcoal-grilling method is preferable to deep-frying and the overall fat profile is lower than fried chicken preparations, making occasional moderate consumption acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Charcoal grilling produces lower proinflammatory AGEs than deep-frying; turmeric in spice paste provides curcumin
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Charcoal-grilling produces lower levels of proinflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) compared to deep-frying, and the turmeric in the spice paste provides curcumin with potential anti-inflammatory properties, making Ayam Percik a preferable choice over fried chicken for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Percik is a celebrated Malaysian grilled chicken dish originating from the east coast states of Kelantan and Terengganu. The name 'percik' means to sprinkle or baste in Malay, referring to the defining technique of repeatedly splashing the chicken with a thick, rich coconut milk sauce while it grills slowly over charcoal. The chicken is first marinated in a spice paste of lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, chilli, shallots, garlic, and ginger, then slow-grilled over charcoal while being basted continuously with a sauce made from thick coconut milk, tamarind, and palm sugar. This repeated basting caramelises the coconut milk on the chicken skin, forming a golden, aromatic crust with a distinct sweet-smoky flavour. The result is a succulent, mildly spicy chicken with a luscious caramelised coating that is intermediate in fat content between deep-fried and plain-grilled preparations. Nutritionally, Ayam Percik provides 198 kcal per 100g, with 18.5g protein, 12.5g fat (5.2g saturated from coconut milk), and only 4.2g carbohydrate. The low carbohydrate content and high protein make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The sodium content of 390mg/100g warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. It is a star attraction at Ramadan bazaars and is typically served with white rice, Nasi Kerabu, or Nasi Dagang. It is classified as halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD (nutriweb.org.my), an approved primary regional source for Malaysian dishes. The record is an exact match for Ayam Percik in the Malaysian food composition database. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are supplemented from enriched JSON with confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate at confidence 0.7, which is the best available evidence given the absence of a direct GI measurement for this dish.
notes: Trans fat is 0.0g per 100g as confirmed by the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source; this is a confirmed zero for a non-hydrogenated grilled preparation. Monounsaturated fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are -1.0 because the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source does not report these specific fatty acid breakdowns. Water_g is -1.0 because the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source does not report water content. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to coconut-milk-based grilled chicken; no direct GI measurement for Ayam Percik exists in the published literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 4.2g × 2.5 serving factor) / 100 ≈ 1.9. Two portions in the input had duplicate is_default=true; the canonical serving of 250g (half chicken, bone-in, grilled) is retained. FoodOn URI is null as no specific FoodOn concept exists for Ayam Percik; closest parent noted. FoodEx2 code A0CHF used as closest defensible parent.
